metadata.summary: Five-stage framework: Define success metric, Measure health, Engage via behavior, Enable self-serve, Expand. Core rule: cap re-engagement to 1/week per account, never auto-issue refunds.

content.title: Customer Success Basics for Solopreneurs
content.summary: A proactive framework for ensuring customers achieve their goals with your product before they churn. Five stages: Define (one north-star success metric per product), Measure (per-account health score from usage + sentiment + billing), Engage (event-triggered lifecycle touchpoints, not calendar dates), Enable (knowledge base + self-serve resources built before lifecycle emails), Expand (upgrade signals → expansion prompts). Core rule: trigger touchpoints on behavior events, cap automated re-engagements at one per week per account, and never let an agent issue refunds, downgrades, or credits autonomously.

why.p: Reactive support addresses problems after they cause churn. Customer success cuts churn by catching low-usage, low-adoption, and low-sentiment signals before the cancellation decision is made. For SaaS, reducing monthly churn from 5% to 3% doubles LTV. Expansion (net revenue retention above 100%) is only reachable when customers are genuinely successful — expansion motions fail on unsuccessful accounts.

list.item: Defining success metrics for the first time on a SaaS, course, membership, or service product
Building lifecycle email sequences (Day 1/3/7/14/30) with product-event triggers
Computing customer health scores and routing at-risk accounts
Designing self-serve enablement: knowledge base, onboarding guides, video library
Building expansion playbooks: detect upgrade signals, trigger relevant offers
Detailed health-score modeling and CS KPIs — use ops-customer-success-metrics
Churn-prevention forensics on already-churning customers — different, deeper diagnostic
Enterprise named-account QBRs — framework covers structure; agents should not drive strategic conversations
Pre-sales / SDR motion — use gtm-strategy or growth-cold-outreach

section.name: success-definition

rule.statement: Define exactly one north-star success metric per product before building any CS motion. "Active projects per team >= 3" is a success metric. "Customer engagement" is not.
rule.rationale: Multiple success metrics create measurement ambiguity and split CS effort. A single north-star forces clarity about what "successful customer" means and makes health scoring objective.

section.p: SaaS tool: success = using core features; metric = feature adoption rate or DAU/seat. Online course: success = completing content + implementing learnings; metric = completion rate (target 80%+). Service: success = achieving stated outcome; metric = results delivered vs promised. Membership: success = engaging regularly; metric = engagement score (logins, content consumption).

section.name: touchpoint-design

rule.statement: Trigger lifecycle touchpoints on product behavior events, not on fixed calendar days. "Day-7 email" sent when a customer has not yet logged in a single time is noise, not success.
rule.rationale: Event-triggered outreach reaches customers when they are in a relevant product context. Calendar-triggered outreach reaches customers regardless of state, producing high unsubscribe rates and trained ignoring.

section.p: Touchpoint cadence: New customer — Day 1 welcome → Day 3 check-in (if no key action) → Day 7 progress → Day 14 feature tip → Day 30 success review. No login for 7 days: re-engagement email with one specific tip tied to their last action. No login for 14 days: personal outreach from founder/CSM. Usage approaching plan limit: proactive upgrade offer with value framing. First success milestone: celebrate + identify next goal + natural expansion conversation.

section.name: segmentation
section.p: Enterprise (high MRR): high-touch — dedicated success manager, QBRs, custom onboarding. Mid-market: medium-touch — periodic calls + automation. SMB: low-touch — mostly automated sequences with human escalation triggers. Self-serve: tech-touch — fully automated, knowledge base, community.

rule.statement: Build the knowledge base before building lifecycle email sequences. Emails should link into existing content; each send must not introduce new content that exists only in the email.
rule.rationale: Customers who click an email link and find no supporting content lose trust. The knowledge base is the product-side capability; email is the notification layer. Build in that order.

example.title: SaaS team collaboration tool
example.note: Success definition: team uses daily, 3+ active projects, 80%+ adoption. Playbook: Day 1 admin onboarding call; Day 7 team training webinar; Day 14 check-in email; Day 30 success review call; Monthly usage report; Quarterly business review. All touchpoints event-triggered where possible (e.g. Day 7 webinar invite only if team has less than 3 projects).

example.title: Online course
example.note: Success definition: complete 80% of content, implement learnings, achieve stated goal. Playbook: Day 1 welcome + learning roadmap; Day 3 first module completion reminder (if not started); Weekly progress email; Module completion acknowledgment + next steps; Completion survey + testimonial request.

section.name: at-risk-intervention

rule.statement: Cap automated re-engagement outreach at one message per week per account. An at-risk account must not receive more than three automated re-engagements before the sequence pauses and escalates to human review.
rule.rationale: Re-engagement spam is the fastest way to convert a disengaged customer into a churned customer who also unsubscribes and leaves a negative review. Automation helps at low volume; at high frequency it accelerates the outcome you are trying to prevent.

section.p: Health band red (score below 50): immediate CSM review, personal outreach within 24 hours. Health band yellow (score 50-74): automated email with specific tip, monitor for 7 days, escalate if no improvement. Health band green (score 75+): maintain regular cadence, watch for expansion signals.

section.name: expansion-path
section.p: Customer hitting plan limits → proactive upgrade offer before they feel blocked. Adding team members → invite prompt for additional seats. Using advanced features → showcase premium tier that extends those features. Requesting a feature → show existing solution + note if upgrade unlocks more. Success milestone reached → celebrate + cross-sell related product.

rule.statement: Never let an agent issue refunds, plan downgrades, comp credits, or any billing action autonomously. Human approval is required at every billing touchpoint.
rule.rationale: Autonomous billing actions are irreversible, create accounting inconsistencies, and may violate subscription terms or payment processor rules. The cost of a single incorrect autonomous credit exceeds any automation efficiency gain.

rule.statement: Agent-written upsell prompts must include the customer's current plan in context before generating the prompt. Never offer a plan the customer is already on.
rule.rationale: An upsell email offering the customer's existing plan reads as broken and damages trust. This is a common failure when agent context does not include current subscription state.

rule.statement: Auto-generated case studies require explicit customer approval before publication. Never publish without a recorded approval step in the workflow.
rule.rationale: Publishing a customer's name, metrics, or story without consent creates privacy violations, potential NDA breaches, and reputational damage. Approval is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: When computing sentiment from support tickets or NPS, always include a confidence score. Route confidence below 0.7 to human review. Sarcasm, code-switching, and short responses make LLM sentiment brittle.
rule.rationale: A customer who writes "wow, great job" after a billing error is probably being sarcastic. LLMs score this as positive. False-positive health scores mask real churn risk.

rule.statement: Re-calibrate health-score thresholds after every significant product release. Rules built on pre-release behavior patterns fire incorrectly after the product changes.
rule.rationale: A health rule that fires when a customer has not used "feature X" becomes incorrect after feature X is removed or renamed. Stale rules produce wrong alerts and wrong suppressions.

rule.statement: Ground every automated outreach in a specific customer action: "you created project X 3 days ago and haven't returned" is personalized. "We noticed you haven't been active" is generic noise.
rule.rationale: Generic empathy trains customers to ignore automated messages. Specific behavioral grounding demonstrates that the system is paying attention and produces higher response rates.

rule.statement: Customer event logs contain PII (names, email, behavioral patterns, company data). Apply field-level redaction before sending to external LLM APIs. Aggregate summaries only through cloud endpoints.
rule.rationale: Product event logs often contain full user identification alongside sensitive behavioral data. Privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA) restrict sending this data to third-party processors without specific legal basis.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Run a continuous CS agent: ingests product-event stream (Mixpanel/Amplitude/Segment), Stripe billing, support tickets, NPS responses; computes per-account health weekly; emits a list of at-risk accounts with suggested outreach drafts and a list of expansion-ripe accounts with upgrade prompts. Hand off all customer-facing communication to a human reviewer for personal touchpoints; let automation handle the templated lifecycle drips. Pair with ops-customer-success-metrics (KPIs), ops-customer-support (tickets), ops-upselling-cross-selling (expansion).

Recommended subagents: faion-growth-agent (source README) — owns CS playbook construction and at-risk triage. faion-content-agent — drafts the lifecycle emails, success guides, knowledge-base entries. improver — quarterly: review which playbook actions actually moved retention and prune ineffective touchpoints. General-purpose Claude subagent — converts raw event logs to per-customer narrative summaries for human CSM review.

Prompt pattern: "For each account in <list>, compute: adoption_score: % of core features used in last 30 days, engagement_score: weekly logins, sentiment_score: from latest NPS + support sentiment, billing_signal: usage approaching plan limit? Output ranked list with reason + suggested next action (email | call | upsell offer | wait)."

Prompt pattern: "Draft a Day-7 check-in email for a customer of <product>. Their usage shows <events>. Tone: helpful, not salesy. Include one specific tip tied to a feature they haven't tried. <120 words. Subject line under 50 chars."

CLI tools: segment-cli (pull/replay events into the CS pipeline), mixpanel-export (pull behavioral events for health scoring), amplitude-cli (same for Amplitude), intercom-cli (drive Intercom messages, tags, segments), customerio-cli (lifecycle email sequences via API), stripe (billing signals), gh (if support is GitHub Issues-based, sync ticket state).

Services: Vitally (API-rich CS platform; good agent fit), Totango (health scoring, playbooks; API surfaced), Gainsight (enterprise; API exists but heavy), ChurnZero (lifecycle automation; integrates with Stripe/Salesforce), Customer.io (best-in-class transactional + lifecycle email API), Intercom (messaging + product tours; Fin AI available), Front (shared inbox; API for routing + tagging), Help Scout (lighter CS/support hybrid; good API), Mixpanel/Amplitude/PostHog (source of truth for usage signals), Notion/GitBook/Document360 (knowledge base; auto-publish docs from agent output), Delighted/Wootric (NPS/CSAT; API for sentiment ingestion), Discourse/Circle/Discord (community surfaces — peer success signal).

Limitations: source README is high-touch/low-touch agnostic; doesn't deeply address tech-touch (fully-automated) flows where most solo SaaS lives. Health scoring suggested but framework not specified — treat as a starting outline only; needs real data + iteration. Engagement cadence ("Day 1, 3, 7, 14, 30") is a default that erodes for B2B with long evaluation cycles. "Define success" is product-specific; the four buckets (SaaS / course / service / membership) miss hybrid models (community + tool, hardware + subscription). Doesn't address negative-churn dynamics, expansion-MRR economics, or net-revenue retention math.

Agents will draft generic empathy; force grounding in the specific customer's events ("you created project X 3 days ago and haven't returned"). LLM-summarized sentiment is brittle — sarcasm, code-switching, and short replies confuse it. Always require a confidence score and route low-confidence to humans. Health-score thresholds drift with product changes; agents will keep firing old rules. Re-calibrate every product release. Auto-generated case studies leak customer details. Mandatory human + customer approval before publication. Re-engagement spam is the #1 way agents damage trust; cap automated outreaches at 1 per week per account. Agent-written upsell prompts that ignore plan boundaries will offer plans the customer is already on; pull current plan into context every time. Do not let an agent issue refunds, plan downgrades, or comp credits autonomously. Human approval required at every billing touchpoint. Privacy: customer event logs include PII. Use field-level redaction before sending to external LLM APIs.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Define one north-star success metric per product, not five. "Active projects per team >= 3" beats "engagement composite." Trigger touchpoints on behavior (event-based), not on calendar dates. Day-7 email when nobody logged in yet is noise. Personalize the variable that matters (their use case, their last action), not the cosmetic ones (first name). Make every touchpoint optional to reply to but easy to escalate; one-way drips train customers to ignore you. Build the knowledge base before the lifecycle emails; emails should link into existing content, not introduce new content per send. Celebrate customer wins publicly (with permission) — case studies double as expansion fuel. Set "do not contact" cooldowns; an at-risk account should not get three automated re-engagements in seven days. Measure NPS, but treat it as a relative trend, not an absolute. Cohorted NPS over time beats company-wide NPS snapshot.

template.purpose: Minimal health score: usage (40), adoption (30), sentiment (20), billing (10) → score + band
Customer success playbook: onboarding table, ongoing triggers, at-risk intervention
